blocks.q: What is a Mental Health Crisis?
blocks.a:
A mental health crisis is when someone’s actions put them in danger. Or, when they can’t care for themselves or function well in their community. People can call for themselves, or for people they’re worried about.
If you feel unsafe, like you might hurt yourself or someone else, please reach out to Crisis Support Services.

blocks.a: Crisis Support Services connect you with an advocate who can help you make a plan to stay safe. They can send an advocate to you in-person to keep you safe. They can also connect you with more support.

blocks.q: What Happens when I call a Crisis Line?
blocks.a:
When you call a Crisis Line, you may have to make choices in an automated menu. Services are free & confidential. Advocates help with safety planning, emotional support, and/or connecting to emergency services.
You can share as little or as much information as you want. You are in control of the call.

blocks.title: ON FIRE — Immediate Steps
blocks.textHTML:
Shut it Down: Sleep (no overdosing). Can’t sleep? Cold shower or face in ice-water (30 seconds and repeat). This is a reset button. It slows everything way down.

No Important Decisions: Especially deciding to die. Do not panic. Ignore thoughts that you don’t care if you die. Stop drugs and alcohol.

Make Eye Contact: A difficult but powerful pain reliever. Look in their eyes and say “Can you help me get out of my head?” Try video chat. Keep trying until you find someone.

blocks.a:
DV is a pattern of behaviors. It’s used by one partner to keep power over another person in a relationship. DV comes in many forms: Physical Abuse, Emotional Verbal Abuse, Sexual Abuse, Financial Abuse, Digital Abuse, and Stalking.
You deserve to feel safe. You have the right to say “no”. If someone is making you feel unsafe, hurting you, or taking advantage of you, there is help.
Not sure? You can also get support to learn about healthy relationships. This includes setting boundaries and making decisions that are right for you.

blocks.q: What Kind of Resources Are There?
blocks.a:
Hotlines - Connect with someone who can listen to you, explain options, find help, and create safety plans.
Shelters - DV shelters are a safe space that a person can go when they leave DV. Hotlines refer directly to shelters when needed.
Advocates - Can help you learn about your options and make a plan. They can go with you to a police station, court, or appointments.
Classes / Groups - Help you learn about DV, healthy relationships, boundaries, financial independence, and more.

blocks.title: How Do I Know if My Relationship is Healthy?
blocks.text:
All relationships fall somewhere on a spectrum from healthy to unhealthy to abusive. No relationship is perfect and all couples go through tough times. But overall, healthy relationships make us feel good. Unhealthy and abusive ones don't.
Healthy BehaviorCommunicating: Both can express how they feel and feel safe. Both listen and respect what the other is saying.
Trusting: Both believe what the other says. Both have evidence of honesty. Both can share things with each other.
Emotionally Respectful: Both respect likes, dislikes, thoughts, and ideas that are important to the other person.
Physically Respectful: Both express how they do and do not want to be touched. Both respect boundaries.
Spending Time: Both enjoy being around the other. Both enjoy and can spend time with other people or alone.
Unhealthy Behavior
Not Communicating: When a problem comes up, partners fight or don’t talk about the problem at all.
Not Trusting: One partner doesn’t believe what the other says or respect the other person’s privacy.
Trying to Control: One partner feels their likes and dislikes are more important the other person’s.
Physically Pressuring: Partner uses guilt or pressures the other to let them touch them in ways they do not like.
Controlling Time: Time is spent only with each other, or only with people that one partner decides.
Abusive Behavior
Hurtful Communication: Threatens, insults, or breaks the other down when problems come up.
Accusing: One partner covers up dishonesty and accuses the other of dishonesty or cheating when it is not true.
Denies Abuse: Partner blames the other for their own actions, makes excuses, or minimizes the abusive behavior.
Physically Harmful: One partner hits, kicks, or harms the other. This may also include sexual violence.
Isolation: Partner controls where the other goes and who they talk to. Limits time with family or friends.

blocks.colLeft:
Plan & Prepare

  • Make a plan with your children so they stay safe
  • Arrange for a place to stay when you have to leave
  • Hide copies of important papers, if safe
  • Collect important phone numbers
  • Select a “code word” that alerts others to call the police to get help
  • Leave suitcase and essential items with a friend
  • Talk to someone you trust about what has been happening.


During an Incident

  • Get out if you can and call 911
  • Keep your phone with you
  • Stay away from:
    • Rooms with weapons (kitchen, bathroom)
    • Rooms with one exit
    • Small spaces
blocks.colRight:
Safety at Home: After You Leave

  • Change locks on doors
  • Screen your calls
  • Notify trusted friends and family if needed
  • Consider filing a police report
  • Consider order of protection

Safety Online

  • Use Private Browsing Mode
  • Clear Cache & Cookies
  • Lock devices with passcode
    (not face ID or fingerprint)

Can’t Leave or Are Not Ready?

  • Learn where you can get help
  • Do what you can to stay safe
  • Call a hotline for support

SNAP / Food Stamps (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
Can help you and your family access healthy food options. SNAP is a federal program that provides financial assistance to eligible individuals and families, helping them purchase groceries. 

WIC (Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children) 
supports the health and nutrition of pregnant women, new mothers, infants, and young children. WIC is a federal assistance program that offers nutritious food, nutrition education, breastfeeding support, and access to health care services for eligible families, promoting the well-being of vulnerable populations.

blocks.a:
Applying for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) in can help you and your family access healthy food options. SNAP is a federal program that provides financial assistance to eligible individuals and families, helping them purchase groceries. Here’s a quick guide to help you understand the process:
EligibilityTo qualify for SNAP benefits, you must meet certain income and resource guidelines. The program considers household size, income, and expenses such as rent and utilities. Additionally, there are work requirements for able-bodied adults without dependents.
How to ApplyOnline Application: The easiest way to apply is through the NY website (mybenefits.ny.gov). It allows you to fill out the application form and submit required documents online.

In-Person: You can also apply in person at your local Department of Social Services (DSS) office. An appointment might be necessary, so it's advisable to call ahead.

Mail or Fax: If you prefer, you can obtain an application form online, print it, and then either mail or fax it to your local DSS office.


Required DocumentsWhen applying, ensure you have the following documents ready:
Proof of identity (e.g., driver's license, passport)
Social Security numbers for all household members
Proof of income (e.g., pay stubs, tax returns)
Proof of residency (e.g., utility bills)
Documentation of expenses

After ApplyingOnce your application is submitted, it will be reviewed, and you might be asked to participate in an interview, either over the phone or in person. If approved, you’ll receive an Electronic Benefit Transfer (EBT) card, which works like a debit card for purchasing eligible food items.
For more information or assistance, visit the New York State Office of Temporary and Disability Assistance website or contact your local DSS office. Remember, applying for SNAP is a step towards ensuring your family has access to nutritious food.

blocks.a: A homeless shelter is a safe place where people without a home can stay. It offers a temporary bed, meals, and support services to help individuals and families get back on their feet. Their goal is to provide comfort, care, and a path to a better, more stable life.

blocks.q: How Can I Get Help if I am Homeless?
blocks.a: Go to your local Department of Social Services (DSS), sometimes called the County Building. Check in and tell the staff that you are seeking shelter.

blocks.q: How Do I Know if I am Eligible for Shelter?
blocks.a:
To find out if you are eligible for assistance, you will need to fill out an application at your local DSS.
You may be asked to spend any money you have available on a hotel before you can be vouchered for shelter for DSS.

blocks.q: What if DSS is Closed and I Need Shelter?
blocks.a:
Reach out to an agency that is local to you under Shelter After Hours & Weekends. You can call anytime, day or night.
Share your living situation with them and they’ll do all they can to find you a safe place to stay right away.

blocks.q: What Will Happen When I Call?
blocks.a:

When you call, tell the person: “I need somewhere to stay tonight” or “I need housing tonight”.
They will ask you for more information, like where you’ve been staying and what kind of help you need. Then they will connect you with an on-call worker from DSS.

Helping you find a specialist and keep caring for you while you wait, like for mental health, substance use, pain, or other health needs.

blocks.q: What Are My Rights as a Tenant in Upstate New York?
blocks.a: A right is a protected claim to how a person is treated and the conditions they are subjected to. Every tenant in Upstate New York has housing rights protected by federal, state, and local laws that help keep their home safe and in good condition, and protect them from unfair treatment or harassment by landlords. You have the right to file a complaint with Fair Housing, Codes, etc.
blocks.aHTML: A right is a protected claim to how a person is treated and the conditions they are subjected to. Every tenant in Upstate New York has housing rights protected by federal, state, and local laws that help keep their home safe and in good condition, and protect them from unfair treatment or harassment by landlords. You have the right to file a complaint with Fair Housing, Codes, etc.

blocks.a:
Make repair requests in writing. Keep a copy for proof.
Emergencies should be reported right away if something is dangerous or damaging. Example: frozen or leaking pipes, gas leak, electrical issues. Landlord must respond to an emergency ASAP.
Problems should be reported as soon as you can. Landlord has one week to respond to regular repairs.

blocks.a: Collect photos or videos of the problem, save your repair request and messages, get notes from other tenants, and write down what happened and when. You have the right to file a complaint with Codes or seek counsel with Legal Aid.

blocks.q: What if I have safety concerns where I live now?
blocks.a: Make sure to keep notes on when you've talked to, texted, or emailed your landlord to request repairs. If talking to your landlord isn't working, you have options.

blocks.a:
Landlords can only enter a tenant's living space for:
Inspections: 24 hour notice at Move In/Out and Routine up to twice a year. You can request virtual/video inspections.
Showings: 24 hour notice to show space to new tenants or buyers. You can request virtual/video.
Regular Repair: 1 week notice to make requested repairs. You can agree to making the repair sooner.
Emergency Repair: Landlords can go in without notice to fix problems that could cause serious damage or are not safe.

blocks.a:

Charging higher prices based on protected characteristics
Rejecting tenants with children or legal income sources like Section 8
Renting specific locations based on characteristics
Refusing service or emotional support animals
Refusing to rent to domestic violence victims

blocks.a:
A lease is a formal agreement between the tenant and the property owner. A lease explains what both the tenant and owner can expect from each other. Once signed, a lease can't be changed until it ends.
A written lease is best. A spoken agreement may not offer legal protection for you or the property owner.
A lease should clearly state:
Who will live there (the tenant)
The rent amount and any extra fees
When rent is due and who to pay
How long the stay will last
What is allowed and not allowed on the property
A lease cannot take away or change your tenant rights:
To ask for a jury in court if there's a case about someone getting hurt or things being damaged
To live with your close family and one other person, along with your children
To not use your belongings or furniture as a promise to pay rent
You can ask to see repair costs or receipts for any damage caused by you, people who live with you, or your guests before you pay.
Or take away or change the landlord's responsibilities:
To fix things that have worn out or broken due to normal use or that need maintenance. 
Heat, water, cooking appliances should be working.
Windows and doors should close safely, and lock where needed.
There should be no holes in the floor, walls, or ceiling or other safety issues.

blocks.a:
An eviction is the legal process of a landlord forcing a renter to move out. It is a formal court procedure that must follow specific laws.
Not paying rent: If rent isn't paid, landlords can give a 14-day warning. If the rent still isn't paid after those 14 days, landlords can start the eviction process.
Breaking rules in the lease: If a tenant breaks a rule in the lease, landlords give a 10-day warning to fix the problem. If it's not fixed, they can then give a 30-day warning before trying to make the tenant leave.
Lease is up: If your lease ends and you stay, the landlord must give you 30, 60, or 90 days' notice, depending on how long you lived there.



If you get an Eviction Notice (sometimes called Notice to Quit or similar)

If the judge rules against you, a "warrant of eviction" will be issued.

blocks.aHTML:
An eviction is the legal process of a landlord forcing a renter to move out. It is a formal court procedure that must follow specific laws.
Not paying rent: If rent isn't paid, landlords can give a 14-day warning. If the rent still isn't paid after those 14 days, landlords can start the eviction process.

Breaking rules in the lease: If a tenant breaks a rule in the lease, landlords give a 10-day warning to fix the problem. If it's not fixed, they can then give a 30-day warning before trying to make the tenant leave.

Lease is up: If your lease ends and you stay, the landlord must give you 30, 60, or 90 days' notice, depending on how long you lived there.

blocks.q: What Are My Responsibilities as a Tenant?
blocks.a: Responsibilities are the things you are expected to do or take care of. These help ensure that everyone has a safe and pleasant place to live.
blocks.aHTML: Responsibilities are the things you are expected to do or take care of. These help ensure that everyone has a safe and pleasant place to live.

blocks.a:
Being a good tenant helps protect your housing and your relationship with your landlord. It also protects your rights if problems come up. You don't have to be perfect — just do your best to stay on top of things.

Pay Rent on Time

Pay your rent by the date in your lease. If you're going to be late, let your landlord know as soon as you can. Keep a record of every payment — a receipt, a money order stub, or a bank statement. This protects you if there's ever a disagreement.

Take Care of Your Space

Keep your home reasonably clean and take out the trash. If something breaks or stops working, report it to your landlord right away. Small problems can turn into big ones if they're not fixed early. You're not expected to fix things yourself — but letting your landlord know is your responsibility.

Follow Your Lease

Your lease is an agreement between you and your landlord. It covers things like how many people can live there, whether pets are allowed, and rules about noise. If you're not sure what's in your lease, read through it or ask someone to help you understand it.

Be a Good Neighbor

Keep noise at a reasonable level, especially at night. Be respectful of shared spaces like hallways, laundry rooms, and parking areas. If you have a problem with a neighbor, try to talk it out calmly or ask your landlord for help.

Know Your Rights

Being a good tenant doesn't mean accepting everything. You have the right to a safe, livable home. If your landlord isn't making repairs, entering your space without notice, or treating you unfairly, you can get help. Our Renter/Tenant Rights resource sheet has more information.

Ask for Help if You Need It

If you're struggling with rent, facing eviction, or not sure what to do, talk to any staff at CHBS. We can help connect you with resources and support.

blocks.a:
Paying the full rent on time, as agreed upon in the lease, is super important. Remember, there's a 5-day grace period before it's considered late.
Ways to Pay:
Cashier's check or bank draft — Your bank writes the check, and they make sure the money is taken from your account first. So, no bounce-backs!
Certified check — Your bank checks that you have enough money before your landlord cashes your check.
Money order — You pay the full amount and a small fee. It's safe, secure, and can be purchased at many places like banks, the post office, or even your local grocery store.
However you pay rent, always get a receipt!

blocks.q: Tips for Keeping Your Space Clean
blocks.a:
A clean home can help you feel better and stay healthy. You don't have to do it all at once. Pick what you can, when you can.

A Few Habits That Help

Try to clean up a little as you go — it adds up. Washing dishes before bed keeps the kitchen from getting overwhelming. If laundry piles up, try doing a small load when you can. If you have things you don't use, donating them can free up space.

Every Day (or Most Days)

Wash dirty dishes, wipe down counters and the kitchen sink, sweep or vacuum floors you use the most, and put a load of laundry in if needed.

Once a Week

Change your sheets, throw out old food, wipe down doorknobs and light switches, clean mirrors, vacuum floors and furniture, clean the toilet and bathroom floor, wipe down the stove and kitchen appliances, and scrub the shower or tub.

A Couple Times a Year

Wipe down blinds, curtains, vents, light fixtures, and fans. Clean baseboards, shake out rugs, scrub tile or grout, clean inside the fridge and freezer, and clean your coffee maker if you have one.

Once a Year (When You Can)

Clean behind and under big furniture and appliances. Wipe down doors and walls. Clean windows and screens. Clean out the dryer vent. If you're able to, deep clean carpets and upholstery — or ask about getting help with it.

blocks.a: LGBTQIA+ is an abbreviation for lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer or questioning, intersex, asexual, and more. These terms are used to describe a person's sexual orientation or gender identity.
blocks.aHTML: LGBTQIA+ is an abbreviation for lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer or questioning, intersex, asexual, and more. These terms are used to describe a person's sexual orientation or gender identity.

blocks.a: Sexual orientation is about who you are attracted to and who you want to have relationships with. It can include being attracted to the same gender, a different gender, or multiple genders. Everyone's experience is unique, and it's a natural part of who you are.
blocks.aHTML: Sexual orientation is about who you are attracted to and who you want to have relationships with. It can include being attracted to the same gender, a different gender, or multiple genders. Everyone's experience is unique, and it's a natural part of who you are.

blocks.a: Gender identity is a deeply personal sense of who you are as a person in terms of gender. It may be male, female, a blend of both, neither, or something else entirely. It may or may not match the sex you were assigned at birth. Your gender identity reflects how you feel inside and how you want to express yourself to the world.

blocks.a: Gender-affirming care is a supportive form of healthcare that respects and acknowledges a person's gender identity. blocks.q: What Does it Mean to be an Ally?
blocks.a: Being an ally to the LGBTQIA+ community means standing up for equality, respect, and dignity for everyone, regardless of their sexual orientation or gender identity. It involves listening, learning, and offering support. Allies use their voices to challenge discrimination and promote inclusion, ensuring that all individuals feel safe, valued, and understood.

blocks.q: How Can I Find an LGBTQIA+ Friendly Provider?
